item.title: [FHIR-53484] Evaluate conformance testability for DTR client guidance
item.description:
There are several conformance statements for DTR clients that either are 1) not practically verifiable or 2) unrelated to interoperability.

We should review conformance statements to ensure they are both testable and useful to include in an interoperability spec.

 

These are some statements I'd recommend for review:

--------

1. The DTR client SHALL use result caching so that results that are already available will be reused without requesting them again.

Recommendation:  Remove this statement entirely.  How a DTR client chooses to optimize their pre-pop logic is an internal implementation detail.  

------

2. The answers to questions populated by an initialExpression or calculatedExpression might themselves be dependencies to determine whether a Questionnaire item is enabled or not, which MAY in turn influence whether additional pre-population is required. 

Note: I don't think the MAY here is actually a conformance verb.  It is just a statement of fact.  Unless we are saying Questionnaire authors MAY design questionnaires in this manner?  In which case we should clarify that this conformance statement is for Questionnaire authors, not DTR clients.

------

3. DTR clients SHALL iterate as necessary until a steady state is reached. If dependencies are such that a steady state cannot be reached (e.g., an item that is enabled causes a value to be set which causes a different item to be enabled, that then disables the first item...), then the Questionnaire SHALL be treated as erroneous and attempts at automatic population SHALL end, with the user being informed of that.

Recommendation:  I think this is basically saying "don't get into infinite loops".  While I agree that is a good idea, I think this should be phrased in the form of informative guidance rather than conformance.  Proposed update:

Questionnaires may be designed such that  answers to questions populated by an initialExpression or calculatedExpression might result in a Questionnaire that does not enter a steady state when executed.  For example, an item that is enabled may cause a value to be set which causes a different item to be enabled, that then disables the first item.  In this situation, the Questionnaire may be treated as erroneous.

------

4. The DTR client SHALL retrieve the FHIR resources specified in the dataRequirement section of a Library.

Recommendation: This is an implementation detail and should be removed.  It may be that a DTR client transforms CQL into another domain specific language and runs that logic on internal data, rather than FHIR.  This is a well understood pattern in other areas of CQL. 

------

5. Queries SHOULD be constructed to minimize the retrieval of information that is not necessary to answer the relevant questions (For example, queries for medications that only require active medications should have appropriate filters to retrieve active medications and not inactive medications). It's possible not every CQL statement will be executed (for example some questions may only be enabled given certain answers to prior questions). To reduce data transfers and increase overall speed, data MAY be fetched as needed. However, the app's execution engine MAY be implemented using a different strategy (for example by doing bulk fetches before starting execution).

Recommendation:  Reframe this as guidance.  Query optimization is a complicated topic, and will, in practice, necessarily be an internal implementation detail.  We might provide guidance that a DTR client might inspect all dataRequirements for all Libraries, and then generate an optimized query that gathers as much data in as few queries as possible. But since we do not describe how to optimize such queries, conformance language doesn't seem appropriate.

6. An adaptive form handles population slightly differently from a 'standard' form because questions only become available one (or a few) at a time. Some CQL Libraries MAY themselves only become available once certain questions are displayed.

Note:   Is this MAY a conformance verb, or just a statement of fact?  Also, how could library availability be tied to display?  If this were to be conformance language, would it be tied to questionnaire retrieval, not display?  Also, if this is conformance, it is on the Adaptive questionnaire server, not the DTR client, right?  

 

------

7. DTR clients SHALL support such an incremental population of adaptive QuestionnaireResponses. For performance, the DTR application SHOULD save the results of execution of CQL prior to the $next-question call.

Note:  The SHALL here seems... obvious?  Is there any other option for a DTR client?  I guess I don't object to keeping that though, since anything else would basically just be "don't do form prepop for adaptive at all".

Recommendation:  Remove the SHOULD.  Performance optimization is an internal implementation detail of the DTR client. 

------

8. If DTR is launched using a previously saved QuestionnaireResponse, the DTR client SHOULD re-execute CQL to populate all empty elements, as well as those with an origin.source of 'auto-client' or 'auto-server'.

Question:  this might just be me misunderstanding, but should the DTR client execute CQL to pre-pop items that are 'auto-server'?

9. The FHIR Library containing/referencing a CQL logic file can reference other needed CQL files (e.g., helper libraries) using the relatedArtifact field and a RelatedArtifact with a type of depends-on. The engine SHALL make available to the execution context all such referenced CQL Libraries.

Recommendation:  make this informative guidance rather than conformance language.  This feels like an internal implementation detail.  In the  case where CQL is converted to a DSL, it might not be the CQL Library that is passed to the execution context.

------

10. If the CQL is malformed (is not syntactically correct) in any way, the app's execution engine SHALL NOT attempt any execution of the malformed CQL. If the CQL engine reports an error when executing any of the CQL the app should similarly stop all CQL execution.

This assumes a CQL engine rather than a DSL engine or other option.  Reframe this as guidance.

11. In either case, the client SHALL report the error to the payer using the Log Questionnaire Errors operation and SHOULD Log the error internally and/or in the DTR client if possible.

Recommendation;  remove the SHOULD about logging errors.  Internal error logging is an implementation detail - it has nothing to do with interoperability.  However, the first conformance statement around reporting the error the payer is an excellent example of a conformance statement, since it directly has to do with interoperability, and should be kept.

12. If the CQL in the Questionnaire is only used for form population purposes, the app SHALL:

  • Notify the user with an appropriate message indicating that the form population did not occur.
  • Allow the user to enter the information manually, either now or at a later time.

Recommendation:  Convert this to guidance.  The user interface behavior is not something that will be practically conformance testable.

 

---- 

13. If the CQL also includes questionnaire logic, such as whether certain elements are enabled, or other validation rules, the app SHALL notify the user that there is an issue with the questionnaire and prohibit further completion of the form.
 
Question:  do we really want to prohibit completion of the form just because of CQL errors?
 
 
------
14. The developer of SMART on FHIR DTR apps SHALL define an endpoint maintaining a list of payers currently supported by that app.
 
Comment:  The developer of the DTR app isn't the one that has the list of payers, it is the operator. 
 
---- 
15. It is possible that additional properties MAY be supported in the future.

Note:  This doesn't seem like a conformance statement. It is a statement of fact.  If it is conformance, then it seems like this is a requirement for.. the IG authors?

16. At a minimum, the app and the HIT server SHALL support access to the following resources via the 21st Century Cures Act compliant APIs:

Note:  We should be more specific here.  the 21st Century Cures Act was legislation.  That legislation spawned many rules, including the 21st Century Cures Rule, HTI-1, HTI-2, etc.   Those rules defined many APIs.  I think what we mean here is US Core compliant APIs?  Though some of the resources listed are not covered by US Core.

 

—

17. Resources referenced from the above via Must Support elements, including transitive references, SHALL also be supported.

Note:  Is this "Must Support, the US Core definition or the DTR definition?  Also, this contradicts US Core, assuming the intent of the 21CCA APIs meant US Core APIs.  US Core does not require that all MS elements be supported.  

---- 

I might have more to add in the comments.
item.summary: Evaluate conformance testability for DTR client guidance

status.description: All of the agreed changes have been applied to the specification

comments.comment: https://confluence.hl7.org/spaces/CQIWC/pages/413048861/Tue+Q2+2026-01-27+CDS+Hosting+CQI
Reviewed and approved by BR on 1/22/2026

I updated the comment to add numbers to the quoted sections for easier cross-referencing.

  1. It's probably reasonable to drop conformance language here, but keeping it as a recommendation is important to highlight that it's often going to be necessary to maintain adequate conformance.
  2. We should change MAY to 'might'.
  3. The expectation to iterate, to check for loops, and how to behave if a loop is detected are all SHALL conformance requirements.  A system that doesn't iterate, doesn't check for loops, or doesn't behave consistently when loops are detected would be non-conformant, so conformance language is definitely needed here.  It may be possible to express the requirements using cleaner language, but that language would still need conformance verbs.
  4. We can reword slightly to "The DTR client SHALL make the content of the FHIR resources specified in the dataRequirement section of a Library available for use when evaluating questionnaire flow control and population logic".  That gives more flexibility about how you implement, without removing the implementation requirement.
  5. The SHOULD is still appropriate conformance language.  The MAYs aren't necessary.
  6. MAY should be 'might'
  7. It ought to be obvious, but it's also a significant complication, so calling out the requirement is appropriate.  Happy to change the SHOULD to "For performance, the DTR client may find it helpful to cache the results of CQL prior to the $next-question call." as it is just implementation guidance, not something to evaluate conformance on
  8. For group discussion
  9. How is this internal implementation?  If the requirement is "must take into account the CQL when populating/performing logic", that's all of the CQL - including the content referenced in libraries.  "Taking into account" may sometimes mean ignoring if you look at it and decide you don't care - but you can't decide you don't care without looking.
  10. For group discussion.  If the CQL is badly written, it's not clear it's safe for AI to use either.
  11. Requirements for internal logging is something that can legitimately be a conformance requirement.  However, agree that what's listed here isn't something we'd expect conformance validation around.  Change to "and log as per their usual error handling conventions".  I.e. we're not upping the bar here.
  12. UI can absolutely be conformance tested.  It's a pain to do so, but it can be done.  And there are lots of IGs that set conformance expectations for UI.  The requirements being set here are reasonable and appropriate to ensure the DTR client is meeting the expectations of the clinical users.
  13. If there are errors that mean it's not possible to know what questions should be displayed, then yes.  The form is broken.  Getting the user to fill it out is likely to waste their time or prompt them for information that's nonsensical.
  14. It's the app.  The app will have a way to establish trusted communication to the payer or it won't.  The EHR and payer both have to choose to trust the app.  The EHR doesn't have to establish a trust relationship to the payer because it doesn't communicate directly with the payer.
  15. Change MAY to might
  16. For discussion
  17. It means anything flagged as MS whether in a BR or US Core profile.  This isn't changing the mustSupport rules for an element.  If US Core says "support if you have it", that still applies.  However, if you have it and it's MS and it's a reference, this requirement says the referenced resource SHALL also be accessible via the API.  If you have an MS that's a reference to a PractitionerRole and you don't expose an endpoint to allow retrieval of that PractitionerRole, you'd be non-conformant.

customfieldvalues.customfieldvalue:
1. Will remove "The DTR client SHALL use result caching so that results that are already available will be reused without requesting them again."  Will instead add "The DTR client SHOULD use result caching so that results queried by CQL (or otherwise retrieved based on CQL) previously remain available after a subsequent call to $next-question."
  2. Will change " which MAY in turn influence" to "" which might in turn influence"
  3. Will leave as is.  Iteration is a required behavior.  Exiting a looping iteration is also a required behavior.  Failure to do either of these things will result in incorrect population, or worse, a hung process.  Many implementers won't know this needs to happen without being explicitly told.  And it's something that should be tested.
  4. Will change "The DTR client SHALL retrieve the FHIR resources specified in the dataRequirement section of a Library." to "If executing CQL directly, the DTR client SHALL retrieve the FHIR resources specified in the dataRequirement section of a Library."  (If this isn't done, the CQL won't run, and not all implementers will recognize this is a requirement if it's not spelled out here.)
  5. Will move this to the implementation guidance section.  Will change "data MAY be fetched" to "data can be fetched" and " engine MAY be implemented" to " engine might be implemented"
  6. Will change "Some CQL Libraries MAY themselves" to "Some CQL Libraries might themselves"
  7. While it would be nice if we could count on 'obvious', experience shows that being explicit is often necessary.  However, will change "For performance, the DTR application SHOULD save the results of execution of CQL prior to the $next-question call." to "For performance, the DTR client may find it helpful to cache the results of CQL prior to the $next-question call."
  8. Will add "When executing CQL for population, DTR clients SHALL replace payer-prepopulated data unless the results of the CQL population is an empty set."  Will add a note to implementers saying "It is possible that future versions of this specification may allow greater configuration of how population by both payer and provider systems should be managed, for example supporting merger of population data from each.  Implementers are encouraged to share requirements around population where the current rules are inadequate/inappropriate."
  9. Will change "The engine SHALL make available to the execution context all such referenced CQL Libraries." to "If executing CQL directly, the engine SHALL make available to the execution context all such referenced CQL Libraries.  If not executing the CQL directly, alternative processes SHOULD consider the content of referenced libraries as well."
  10. Will change "If the CQL is malformed (is not syntactically correct) in any way, the app's execution engine SHALL NOT attempt any execution of the malformed CQL. If the CQL engine reports an error when executing any of the CQL the app should similarly stop all CQL execution." to "If the CQL is malformed (is not syntactically correct) in any way, a client directly executing the CQL  SHALL NOT attempt any execution of the malformed CQL. If the CQL engine reports an error when executing any of the CQL the client should similarly stop all CQL execution.  Clients not executing the CQL SHOULD be tolerant of malformed CQL."
  11. Will change "and SHOULD Log the error internally and/or in the DTR client if possible" to "in addition to performing its own internal logging processes"
  12. Will not make a change.  It is a safety issue for a user to know that population did not occur, as if they are in the habit of expecting population, they may incorrectly presume that an element that would normally be populated and is left empty might mean that there is no appropriate data and not bother to check for data.  If they're alerted that population did not occur normally, they'll be more likely to double-check and dig for information to correctly complete the form.  The mechanism of notification is left up to the client, so the impingement on UI design is minimal.
    We will add a note to implementers on the conformance page saying "This implementation guide asserts a number of conformance requirements that apply to user interface and/or internal processing behavior.  Da Vinci is undertaking a review of our approach to expressing such requirements and these may be surfaced differently in a future version of this guide."
  13. Will change "If the CQL also includes questionnaire logic, such as whether certain elements are enabled, or other validation rules, the app SHALL notify the user that there is an issue with the questionnaire and prohibit further completion of the form."
    to
    "If the CQL also includes questionnaire logic, such as whether certain elements are enabled, or other validation rules, the DTR client (potentially in consultation with the user) MAY attempt to determine if the questionnaire is sufficiently simple that it would be reasonable to allow the user to attempt completion with potentially improperly enabled items.  If this is not possible, the client SHALL notify the user that there is an issue with the questionnaire and prohibit further completion of the form."
  14. Will change "The developer of SMART on FHIR DTR apps SHALL define an endpoint maintaining a list of payers currently supported by that app." to "Entities deploying SMART on FHIR DTR apps SHALL define an endpoint maintaining a list of payers currently supported by that app."
  15. Will change "It is possible that additional properties MAY be supported in the future." to "It is possible that additional properties might be supported in the future."
  16. Will change "At a minimum, the app and the HIT server SHALL support access to the following resources via the 21st Century Cures Act compliant APIs" to "At a minimum, the app and the HIT server SHALL support search access to the following resources via their FHIR RESTful API using the profiles defined by or referenced by this guide"
  17. No change necessary.  It means anything flagged as MS whether in a BR or US Core profile.  This isn't changing the mustSupport rules for an element.  If US Core says "support if you have it", that still applies.  However, if you have it and it's MS and it's a reference, this requirement says the referenced resource SHALL also be accessible via the API.  If you have an MS that's a reference to a PractitionerRole and you don't expose an endpoint to allow retrieval of that PractitionerRole, you'd be non-conformant.

item.title: [FHIR-55000] BiologicallyDerivedProduct bindings for productCode and property.type should point to available external code systems
item.description:
BDP.productCode can/should point to ISBT 128 product description codes (https://www.isbt128.org/uri/productdescriptioncode/) as example or extensible bindings. BDP.property.type should point to ISBT 128 data elements (https://www.isbt128.org/uri/) as example or extensible bindings. These changes were voted upon and approved for terminology but never made it into the build.

(Comment 26 - imported by: Ron G. Parker)
item.summary: BiologicallyDerivedProduct bindings for productCode and property.type should point to available external code systems

status.description: An change proposal has been fully categorized and is ready to be actioned

item.title: [FHIR-53250] Calendar duration equivalence has conflicts
item.description:
See discussion here #fhirpath > Transitivity and determinism of equivalence for durations

The following relationships can't all be true, or we end up with a conflict:

1 year ~ 1 'a' ~ 365.25 'd' (the final conversion is defined in ucum)
1 year ~ 365 days ~ 365 'd'

Bryn suggested in his reply that 1 year ~ 365 days is not true.

But there is this sentence: 

Implementations that do support units shall do so as specified by [UCUM] as well as the calendar durations as defined in the toQuantity function.

under 6.1.2.1 quantity equivalence https://build.fhir.org/ig/HL7/FHIRPath/#quantity-equivalence.

Proposal: remove that sentence. As well as adding in the toQuantity documentation that the calendar duration conversion factorrs are only to be used by toQuantity function to explicitly convert to a different duration. They cannot be used implicitly in determining equivalence or equality or in comparison.
item.summary: Calendar duration equivalence has conflicts

status.description: An change proposal has been fully categorized and is ready to be actioned

comments.comment:
We should clarify that conversions that do not involve variable-length periods can be transitive, propose adding the following to the end of the paragraph following the calendar duration conversion table:

Conversions involving variable-length periods (i.e. years or months) must be explicitly performed as listed in the table above, as opposed to conversions involving calendar durations that are equivalent to definite-time durations. For example, 1 year.toQuantity('minutes') is invalid, whereas 1 day.toQuantity('minutes') is valid.

Would it be useful if we move this conversation to fhir chat Yury? I think this discussion has grown out of the scope of this proposal (date arithmetics rather than equivalence) and also that way there might be a bit more visibility.

This does work by the way:

(1 year | 1 months | 1 day).aggregate($total + $this, @2026-01-01)

And you don't need a unit conversion.

The first example in the previous message was not so good. Here is a better example:

@2025-01-01 + (29 day + 0.5 day + 12 hours).toQuantity('days') = @2025-01-31
@2025-01-01 + (29 day + 0.5 day + 12 hours).toQuantity('month') = @2025-02-01

This will probably work in some cases, but we still need a unit:

%list_of_quantities.aggregate($total + $this.toQuantity(%some_unit), %some_date)

I want to show that we might not know which units the durations need to be converted to.
This is because the initial date/time and the durations to be added may be unknown at the time the code is written.
The specification is going to say: "calendar unit conversion must be explicit".
I'm not sure how the user will understand which units to use in the general case:

  • @2025-01-01 + (1 day + 0.5 day + 12 hours).toQuantity('days')

    is not the same as 

    @2025-01-01 + (1 day + 0.5 day + 12 hours).toQuantity('hours')
  • T00:00 + (0.5 day + 1 hour).toQuantity('day')

    is not the same as 

    T00:00 + (0.5 day + 1 hour).toQuantity('hours')

how about using the date as the initial value in the aggregate function:

%list_of_quantities.aggregate($total + $this, %some_date)

I don't understand this point in the resolution description:

1 year = 12 months // null, because calendar unit conversion must be explicit

By "null" do you mean "empty"?

If so, then:

@1973-12-25 + 1 year + 12 month = @1975-12-25 // because all operations are performed sequentally
@1973-12-25 + (1 year + 12 month) // empty, because calendar unit conversion must be explicit

What about %someDate + (Bundle.entry.resource as Observation).value.sum() ?

Should this be rewritten in the following way:
%someDate + (Bundle.entry.resource as Observation).value.tail().aggregate($this.toQuantity('milliseconds')+$total.toQuantity('milliseconds'), value.first().toQuantity('milliseconds'))
or
%someDate + (Bundle.entry.resource as Observation).value.tail().aggregate($this.toQuantity('hours')+$total.toQuantity('hours'), value.first().toQuantity('hours'))
or
%someDate + (Bundle.entry.resource as Observation).value.tail().aggregate($this.toQuantity('years')+$total.toQuantity('years'), value.first().toQuantity('years'))
?

Interesting. My 2c: I am postulating the reason for this:

For precisions above seconds, the decimal portion of the time-valued quantity is ignored, since date/time arithmetic above seconds is performed with calendar duration semantics.

is that it's kinda hard to get it right.... `7.5 days` seems like a straightforward example, but things like `7.3 days` might be trickier with precision... and also add in the complexity of leap seconds and stuff it becomes quite hard to get right. I think this is the reason in modern date time libraries the period normally have integer components:

https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.timespan?view=net-10.0

https://kotlinlang.org/api/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx-datetime/kotlinx.datetime/-date-period/

https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/api/java/time/Period.html

But I also think as is, at least the spec is consistent right now. I think all the examples you gave are consistent with each other, right? If we want to revisit whether 7.5 days is allowed we can open a new separate ticket.

Thanks!

The following expression returns false:

@1973-12-25T00:00:00.000+10:00 + 7.5 days = @1973-12-25T00:00:00.000+10:00 + 7 days + 12 hours

Because "For precisions above seconds, the decimal portion of the time-valued quantity is ignored, since date/time arithmetic above seconds is performed with calendar duration semantics.":

@1973-12-25T00:00:00.000+10:00 + 7.5 days = @1974-01-01T00:00:00.000+10:00
@1973-12-25T00:00:00.000+10:00 + 7 days + 12 hours = 1974-01-01T12:00:00.000+10:00

We also have a test for this:
https://github.com/FHIR/fhir-test-cases/blob/master/r4/fhirpath/tests-fhir-r4.xml#L1189

According to the specification, to convert this expression to logically true, you can do the following:

@1973-12-25T00:00:00.000+10:00 + (7.5 days).toQuantity('hours') = @1973-12-25T00:00:00.000+10:00 + 7 days + 12 hours

Are you going to reconsider this?
Agreed, updated the disposition to reflect this
From an implementation point of view, it's easier that way, because we just treat calendar units up to weeks as ucum units.
Do we want to allow implicit conversion for calendar durations up to weeks?

This issue was posted from the following Zulip thread: https://chat.fhir.org/#narrow/channel/179266-fhirpath/topic/Transitivity.20and.20determinism.20of.20equivalence.20for.20durations/with/558003559

Following up here, this tracker: https://jira.hl7.org/browse/FHIR-28438 resulted in a change through the specification to treat date/time arithmetic "above days" as an error, rather than "above seconds", but there is one spot where this change was missed, specifically Section 6.7 Date/Time Arithmetic (https://build.fhir.org/ig/HL7/FHIRPath/#datetime-arithmetic) still refers to "above seconds", but should be "above days (or weeks)", and there should be a note there to indicate the rationale for that change (that for practical reasons, leap-seconds are not considered).

In addition, in toQuantity(unit), the specification indicates that the "conversion factors are only used when time-valued quantities appear in unanchored calculations", but this is part of the issue raised in this ticket and should also be clarified to indicate that these conversions are only to be performed explicitly, not implicitly.

Note that this is also related to the following CQL tickets:
https://jira.hl7.org/browse/FHIR-53148
https://jira.hl7.org/browse/FHIR-53172

Proposed resolutions for those tickets should align with this one.

Sorry let me rephrase my proposal:

Change:

Implementations that do support units shall do so as specified by [UCUM] as well as the calendar durations as defined in the toQuantity function.

To:

Implementations that do support units shall do so as specified by [UCUM]. The conversion factors defined in toQuantity function are for explicit conversion only and should not be used for equivalence. For example, 1 year ~ 365 days should return false. But 1 year.toQuantity('days') should return 365 days.

This is default title:This is default message and can be changed from field configuration.

customfieldvalues.customfieldvalue:
1. In 6.6 Math, 6.1.1.1 Quantity Equality, 6.1.2.1 Quantity Equivalence, and 6.2 Comparison, change:

> Implementations that do support units shall do so as specified by [UCUM] as well as the calendar durations as defined in the toQuantity function.

To:

> Implementations that do support units shall do so as specified by [UCUM]. Note that calendar unit conversions for months and years must be performed explicitly, as defined in the [toQuantity(unit)] function.

2. In 6.7 Date/Time Arithmetic, change:

> To avoid the potential confusion of calendar-based date/time arithmetic with definite duration date/time arithmetic, FHIRPath defines definite-duration date/time arithmetic for seconds and below, and calendar-based date/time arithmetic for seconds and above. At the second, calendar-based and definite-duration-based date/time arithmetic are identical. If a definite-quantity duration above seconds appears in a date/time arithmetic calculation, the evaluation will end and signal an error to the calling environment.

to:

> To avoid the potential confusion of calendar-based date/time arithmetic with definite duration date/time arithmetic, FHIRPath defines definite-duration date/time arithmetic for days and below, and calendar-based date/time arithmetic for days and above. At the day, calendar-based and definite-duration-based date/time arithmetic are identical. If a definite-quantity duration for years or months appears in a date/time arithmetic calculation, the evaluation will end and signal an error to the calling environment.

And add the following as an info note:

> For all but years and months, calendar durations are both equal and equivalent to the corresponding UCUM definite-time duration unit. Note that due to the possibility of leap seconds, this is not totally accurate, however, for practical reasons, implementations typically ignore leap seconds when performing date/time arithmetic.

3. In 5.5.7.1 toQuantity(unit), change:

> Note that calendar duration conversion factors are only used when time-valued quantities appear in unanchored calculations. See Date/Time Arithmetic for more information on using time-valued quantities in FHIRPath.

to:

> Note that because calendar duration conversion factors are approximate, they must be invoked explicitly using the `toQuantity(unit)` function. Attempts to perform comparisons or arithmetic (other than Date/Time arithmetic) requiring calendar duration conversions result in empty ( {} ). See Date/Time Arithmetic for more information on using time-valued quantities in FHIRPath.

4. Add the following examples to 6.7 Date/Time Arithmetic

1 year = 1 'a' // empty ( {} ), because comparisons between calendar and UCUM definite-time duration units above days (or weeks) result in empty
1 year ~ 1 'a' // true, by definition in equivalent
1 year = 12 months // empty ( {} ), because calendar unit conversion must be explicit
1 year ~ 12 months // false, because equivalent comparisons involving unsupported conversions result in false (rather than empty, like equality would)

1 year + 12 months // empty ( {} ), because calendar unit conversion must be explicit
1 year + 12 'mo' // empty ( {} ), because conversion between UCUM definite-time durations above days (or weeks) and calendar units is not supported
1 year.toQuantity('month') + 12 months // returns 24 months (as adding the same unit)

@2025-11-29 + 1 'a' // error, because date/time arithmetic involving UCUM definite-time durations above days (or weeks) is an error
@2025-11-29 + 1 year // @2026-11-29
@2025-11-29 + 12 'mo' // error, because date/time arithmetic involving UCUM definite-time durations above days (or weeks) is an error
@2025-11-29 + 12 months // @2026-11-29

5. In 4.1.8.1 Time-valued quantities (https://build.fhir.org/ig/HL7/FHIRPath/#time-valued-quantities), change:

> The table above defines the equality/equivalence relationship between calendar and definite duration quantities. For example, 1 year is not comparable to 1 'a' (i.e. results in empty), but it is equivalent to 1 'a'. See Date/Time Arithmetic for more information on using time-valued quantities in FHIRPath.
